initial_claims.description: In Solidity 0.4, reassigning a storage array with `array = new uint[](0)` zeroes every previously occupied storage slot individually, costing 5000 gas per slot. An attacker can call insertNnumbers() repeatedly to grow the array to thousands of elements (each call can push up to ~382 elements before hitting gas limit). Once the array is large enough, clearDOS() will always OOG when attempting to zero all slots, permanently bricking the clear functionality. The contract then has no way to reset state.
initial_claims.evidence: Line: `array = new uint[](0);` in clearDOS() — in Solidity 0.4 this iterates over all existing array slots setting them to zero. With array.length > ~5000 elements, the gas cost exceeds block gas limit (~30M gas / 5000 gas per slot = ~6000 slots max). Attacker calls insertNnumbers(1, 382) repeatedly to grow array past this threshold.

claim.description: In Solidity 0.4, reassigning a storage array with `array = new uint[](0)` zeroes every previously occupied storage slot individually, costing 5000 gas per slot. An attacker can call insertNnumbers() repeatedly to grow the array to thousands of elements (each call can push up to ~382 elements before hitting gas limit). Once the array is large enough, clearDOS() will always OOG when attempting to zero all slots, permanently bricking the clear functionality. The contract then has no way to reset state.
claim.evidence: Line: `array = new uint[](0);` in clearDOS() — in Solidity 0.4 this iterates over all existing array slots setting them to zero. With array.length > ~5000 elements, the gas cost exceeds block gas limit (~30M gas / 5000 gas per slot = ~6000 slots max). Attacker calls insertNnumbers(1, 382) repeatedly to grow array past this threshold.

verdict.reasoning: The vulnerable pattern exists in the contract code. The `insertNnumbers()` function at lines 8-15 allows any caller to grow the `array` storage array without restriction — there is no access control, no cap on array size, and no rate limiting. The `clearDOS()` function at lines 22-27 requires `numElements > 1500` and then executes `array = new uint[](0)` which in Solidity 0.4.x zeroes all previously occupied storage slots individually. In EVM terms, writing a non-zero storage slot to zero costs 5000 gas (SSTORE with zero value in pre-EIP-2929 semantics). With a block gas limit of ~8-15M gas (historical) or ~30M gas (current), an attacker can grow the array to a size where clearing it exceeds the block gas limit. Each `insertNnumbers()` call can add ~382 elements before hitting gas limits. After ~40-80 calls, the array can reach 15,000+ elements, making `clearDOS()` permanently uncallable due to OOG. The `clear()` function only resets `numElements` to 0 but does NOT resize the array, so `array.length` remains large — meaning subsequent `clearDOS()` calls still face the same gas cost. The attack is permissionless, requires no special privileges, and permanently bricks the `clearDOS()` functionality. The harm is to the contract's intended operation (inability to reset state), affecting all users who depend on the contract functioning correctly. The severity is medium rather than high because: (1) the contract holds no funds, (2) the harm is operational/functional rather than financial, and (3) the `clear()` function still works (though it only resets the counter, not the array). The defender's argument is correct that no mitigation exists in the code.
verdict.recommendation: Add access control (onlyOwner) to `insertNnumbers()` to prevent arbitrary array growth. Alternatively, implement a paginated clearing mechanism that clears a fixed number of elements per call. For the array reassignment pattern, use `delete array` instead of `array = new uint[](0)` — though in Solidity 0.4 both have similar gas costs. The fundamental fix is to limit who can call `insertNnumbers()` or cap the maximum array size.

There is no mitigation in the code that blocks this attack path. The contract allows any caller to invoke insertNnumbers() with up to ~382 elements per call, and clearDOS() attempts to zero all storage slots via array reassignment, which in Solidity 0.4 iterates over all existing slots.

The vulnerable pattern exists in the contract code. The `clearDOS()` function at lines 22-27 requires `numElements > 1500` and then executes `array = new uint[](0)` which in Solidity 0.4.x zeroes all previously occupied storage slots individually. In EVM terms, writing a non-zero storage slot to zero costs 5000 gas (SSTORE with zero value in pre-EIP-2929 semantics). Each `insertNnumbers()` call can add ~382 elements before hitting gas limits. After ~40-80 calls, the array can reach 15,000+ elements, making `clearDOS()` permanently uncallable due to OOG. The `clear()` function only resets `numElements` to 0 but does NOT resize the array, so `array.length` remains large — meaning subsequent `clearDOS()` calls still face the same gas cost. The attack is permissionless, requires no special privileges, and permanently bricks the `clearDOS()` functionality. The harm is to the contract's intended operation (inability to reset state), affecting all users who depend on the contract functioning correctly.
